narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways during the admissions review process. The automated system we use to evaluate applications predicted a 54% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which tipped the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor outcomes for both you and the university. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your academic record: your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 school decile ranking. The most influential was your LSAT score of 25.50, well below the typical applicant's 36.70 average, which strongly pushed the model toward forecasting a bar failure because lower scores like yours are historically linked to tougher challenges on the exam. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, slightly under the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some solid college performance. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 3, compared to the average of 5.53, had a moderate pull toward failure, indicating your later high school grades placed you lower in your school's performance distribution than most. Compared to typical students who get approved, your LSAT was a standout weakness that outweighed these other elements, as stronger scorers often sail through the bar despite similar GPAs or deciles. Your Year 1 decile of 5 was right around average, and your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of applicants, so they played smaller roles here. While this is disappointing, especially given your stable background, it highlights where targeted improvements could make a real difference. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average—many students in your position have succeeded by focusing on test prep and strengthening that key signal. You might also reflect on bolstering your later academic showings or gaining relevant experience to build a stronger overall case. With persistence, reapplying after addressing the LSAT could flip this prediction in your favor next time.
